coredata.dc:description:
Bubbles oscillating in the presence of ultrasound is commonly employed in biomedical applications for drug delivery, ultrasound enhanced thrombolysis, and the transport and manipulation of cells. This is possible because bubbles tend to interact with the ultrasound to undergo periodic shape changes known as shape-mode oscillation, concomitant with the generation of liquid agitation or streaming. This phenomenon is examined both experimentally and theoretically on a single bubble at a frequency of (45
±
1) kHz. Effects of ultrasonic frequency and power on the flowfield were explored. Experiments revealed different trends in the development of liquid streaming velocities at different acoustic forcing conditions (5.53, 6.80 and 7.02 Vpp), with lowest (0.5 mm/s) and highest (1.1 mm/s) values of time-averaged mean streaming velocity occurring at 6.80 Vpp and 7.02 Vpp, respectively. Simulations captured the simultaneous evolution of bubble-shapes that helped create flow vortices in the liquid surrounding the bubble. These vortices collectively responsible in generating signature patterns in the liquid for a dominant shape-mode of the bubble, and could also generate localised shear stresses for practical application. The velocity and pressure profiles in the liquid around the bubble confirmed the connection of the applied and reflected soundwaves in driving this phenomenon.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Bubbles oscillating in the presence of ultrasound is commonly employed in biomedical applications for drug delivery, ultrasound enhanced thrombolysis, and the transport and manipulation of cells. This is possible because bubbles tend to interact with the ultrasound to undergo periodic shape changes known as shape-mode oscillation, concomitant with the generation of liquid agitation or streaming. This phenomenon is examined both experimentally and theoretically on a single bubble at a frequency of (45
±
1) kHz. Effects of ultrasonic frequency and power on the flowfield were explored. Experiments revealed different trends in the development of liquid streaming velocities at different acoustic forcing conditions (5.53, 6.80 and 7.02 Vpp), with lowest (0.5 mm/s) and highest (1.1 mm/s) values of time-averaged mean streaming velocity occurring at 6.80 Vpp and 7.02 Vpp, respectively. Simulations captured the simultaneous evolution of bubble-shapes that helped create flow vortices in the liquid surrounding the bubble. These vortices collectively responsible in generating signature patterns in the liquid for a dominant shape-mode of the bubble, and could also generate localised shear stresses for practical application. The velocity and pressure profiles in the liquid around the bubble confirmed the connection of the applied and reflected soundwaves in driving this phenomenon.

ce:section.ce:para:
Bubbles can be generated by irradiating quiescent liquid to ultrasound (sound with frequency
>
20 kHz) in a non-invasive, non-intrusive manner [1]. The ultrasound enables the periodic growth and collapse of the bubbles, commonly known as acoustic cavitation [2,3]. During this process, the bubble shape mostly remains spherical, while exhibiting radial oscillations. However, in certain instances, the oscillating bubble surface further interacts with the irradiated sound waves, to generate a mean flow in the immediately adjacent liquid, known as microstreaming [4]. This is accompanied by the uniform surface dilation of the bubble, referred to as shape mode oscillation [5]. Advanced studies have shown that microstreaming occurs when radial oscillation modes (mode number indicated by n = 0) interact with another shape mode (n
>
1) or the translational mode (n = 1) [6], leading to unique fluid patterns. The dynamics of both the evolving bubble shapes and the associated liquid motion during microstreaming changes very rapidly over microsecond time scale and micrometer spatial scale. This characteristic makes it promising for micro-mixing in food/dairy industries [7] and permeabilization of tissues in biomedical applications [8]. Due to the rapid changes in microstreaming dynamics, experimental studies around single free bubbles pose challenges, as it is difficult to isolate a stable acoustic bubble without any translation instabilities [9]. However, recent investigations [10–13] related to acoustic cavitation-induced shear in a target environment (e.g. algae-based cells, enzymes) have spurred renewed interest to understand the development of highly localized stresses produced by oscillating bubbles for applications in biotechnology and therapeutics.

To capture different flow patterns around a bubble, streak photography was used by Collis et al. [14] on a bubble attached to a solid substrate. However this provided limited quantitative information on the ambient flow-field. A similar setup was used by Leong et al. [15], who investigated changes in streaming velocities around the bubble by varying the head-groups and chain-lengths of surfactants introduced in the solvent around the bubble. These studies [14–16] were all confined to bubbles anchored to a solid boundary (where it is easy to control bubble size and location), thereby allowing the surface tension between the wall and the bubble to interfere with the flow-field features. In these cases, the complex contact-line dynamics caused a non-trivial interaction of shape and volume oscillations exhibited by the bubble. Furthermore, the predominance of spherical harmonics complicated the flow-field [17]. Additionally, a bubble anchored to a wall would likely undergo an asymmetric collapse at the end of its lifetime unlike the spherical collapse of a pulsating unattached bubble [1]. All these studies fail to reveal the accurate representation of the actual flow-field around a free bubble.

Recently, Cleve et al. [18] tried to redress this problem by acoustically trapping bubbles in infinite liquid for the first time. This was achieved through the coalescence of two-laser-induced bubbles to create a single, initially deformed bubble, thereby manually triggering shape oscillations. The use of laser not only changes the components inside the bubble but also the nature of bubble oscillation expected in common sonoreactor systems involving acoustic bubbles. Cleve et al. [9] experimentally investigated streaming velocities (
∼
microns/s) around the bubbles. These were triggered at pressure amplitudes beyond the threshold of onset of shape-mode oscillations (
p
th , n
), where shape oscillation modes are indicated by mode number, n. However, the discussion regarding how the liquid velocities would gradually change as one progresses from the critical pressure (
p
c
, let) to
p
th , n
and beyond is still missing. Simultaneously, it is reported [18] that the same bubble would register a transition from spherical oscillations to shape oscillations, with a gradual increase in applied pressure.

Likewise, theoretical predictions of microstreaming velocities [19–22] were conducted to estimate the evolving bubble shapes and associated liquid velocity during microstreaming. There were two approaches followed for this purpose- the first involved applying was by applying fluid dynamic equations developed through the works of Longuet-Higgins [23] and Spelman and Lauga [24]. The second approach was based on acoustic formulations through the works of Wu and Du [19], later refined by Doinikov et al. [21]. However, some or most of these theories are based on certain assumptions. For example, Longuet-Higgins [23], Spelman and Lauga [24] and Wu and Du [19] neglect surface tension forces at the air/liquid interface and assume that the bubble radius is much smaller compared to the wavelength of incident sound, yet greater relative to the thickness of the viscous boundary layer surrounding the bubble, they also assume that the surrounding bulk liquid is non-heat-conducting. Hence, suitable experiments could help better understand the development of this microstreaming phenomenon (both bubble shape and liquid mean flow) with time under different operating conditions.

To address some of these current gaps, a single air bubble is freely levitated in water using ultrasound standing wave (USW) of frequency
∼
45 kHz, away from any neighbouring wall. Particle image velocimetry (PIV) was carried out to capture the adjacent flow-field surrounding the levitated bubble. Furthermore, direct numerical simulation (DNS) is performed to understand the underlying mechanism. Both the experimental and computational methods used are discussed in the following section.

The liquid used to accommodate the bubble is prepared by degassing Milli-Q water (resistivity 18.2
M Ω - cm
, Merck Millipore, Bayswater, VIC, Australia) using our in-house setup (shown in Fig. 1 ) to a vacuum-level of 0.02 MPa. The setup consists of a vacuum pump (John Morris Scientific Pty. Ltd., Australia) and a cold trap using gaseous nitrogen. These are used in order to reduce the concentration of dissolved gases in the water from the flask, which might otherwise act as sources of bubble nucleation. The liquid is delicately transported to a cubical glass-cell. Care is taken so as not to introduce any gas bubbles inside. This is a standard procedure before carrying out single bubble sonoluminescence experiments [25].

A cubical glass cell with dimensions
( 2.5 × 2.5 × 8 )
cm
3
is fabricated using diamond white glass (Ted pella, Vitrocom) for the walls mounted on a stainless steel bottom, connected to a lead zirconate titanate (PZT) piezo ceramic type transducer. The PZT works in conjunction with the amplifier and function generator. To levitate the bubble in the glass cell filled to a height of 7 cm with partially degassed water. This setup accommodates several USWs at a frequency of approximately (45
±
1) kHz with an accuracy of 1 Hz. The primary acoustic force of the applied ultrasound significantly nullifies buoyancy forces, allowing for an almost stable bubble (see Supplementary video). The experimental setup is summarized in Fig. 2 , where Fig. 2 (a) illustrates the PIV setup for capturing images of the flow-field, while Fig. 2 (b) displays the schematic for bubble levitation along with supporting equipment. The ultrasonic transducer was driven by a Krohn-Hite power amplifier (Model 7500) and a programmable 15 MHz Hameg function generator (HM8131-2). The levitation experiments follow the procedures outlined by Lee at el. [26]. Three different peak-to-peak voltages (Vpp) were applied- 5.53 Vpp, 6.8 Vpp and 7.02 Vpp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Image capture of single bubble and its surroundings
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2 (a) provides details of the PIV arrangement used in this study. It consists of a 532 nm Nd:YAG laser (Evergreen-Big Sky Laser Series: 70 mJ) that is attenuated and double-pulsed at 15 Hz to create a thin 50 micron laser-sheet with the aid of cylindrical and spherical lenses (focal length = −20 cm). A high-speed (PCO Dimax HS4) camera is coupled with a C-mount (10X/0.3 Nikon zoom lens) to operate in double-image mode. These assist in capturing images in tandem with a time-synchronising mechanism (via the pulse generator) with every double image captured at 15 Hz apart (i.e. time interval of 66.66 ms) to process each vectorial map. The time interval between the two images of a pair (double images) was increased till a discernible signal could be captured at a 14.2 ms time interval. 2
μ m
diameter red fluorescent polymer micro-spheres (ThermoFisher Scientific) are used with particle Stokes number, St = 0.0964
<
1, thereby ensuring that particles faithfully trace the flow [27]. Supplementary Text 1: Experiments contains further details of PIV calibration, image processing and St calculation.

Additionally, a camera with a charge coupled device (CCD, DAGMTICCD100) is operated with a microscope. This is to locate and zoom closer to the bubble in order to check any disturbance to the levitated bubble. Also, a stroboscope (a light emitting diode) is fitted to operate in synchronization with the ultrasound frequency. This allows observation of the shape of the bubble levitated at a certain power and frequency combination. This is displayed in Fig. 2 (b).

A 1 mm PVdF needle hydrophone (Precision Acoustics Ltd.) was operated using a DC coupler and further connected to an oscilloscope, in the absence of the bubble. The sensor tip is placed inside the liquid at the anti-nodal location (where the bubble is located) to measure the acoustic pressure amplitude (drive pressure). The location of the sensor tip is confirmed by using the He-Ne laser of Class III and the CCD camera to ensure that it coincides with that point where the bubble was earlier levitated. This is illustrated by the schematic of the setup shown by Fig. 2 (c).

This setup is arranged and operated at the start and end of every experiment, without the bubble but in the presence of ultrasonic forcing. The applied voltages measured from 0.098 to 0.11 MPa of acoustic amplitude. This can be checked from the hydrophone calibration data, provided in Supplementary Text 1: Experiments.
For investigation of bubble-dynamics, CFD simulation has been deemed reliable through the recent works of [28,29].

A three-dimensional domain of dimensions (
2.5 × 7 × 2.5
)
cm
3
is selected to model the fluid domain (x-y-z directions), as illustrated in Fig. 3 (a). This domain consists of several hexahedral wedges, with finer mesh in the middle where the bubble-interaction is computed. This ensures higher resolution, crucial for capturing the evolving shapes of the bubble. The minimum size of an element (
Δ x , Δ y
) is approximately (
≈ 10
μ m
) at and around the centroidal location of the domain. The dimensions of the domain and bubble are selected similar to our sonoreactor setup. This along with other boundary conditions, are explained in detail in Section 2.5.3.

(iii) Farfield: On the side-walls, zero gradient BCs are applied. The top surface is kept open to atmosphere, enabling the formation of a standing wave in the liquid domain after a few
μ s
of operation. This is shown in Fig. 3(b) by the alternate regions of high pressure (red) and low pressure (blue), with white zones indicating the location of nodes of the acoustic standing wave.

The time and space discretizations in the numerical model follow Euler and Gauss linear schemes respectively using OpenFOAM opensource software [30]. The laplacian term is discretized using the Gauss linear uncorrected. The preconditioned conjugate gradient (PCG) method is used to treat the pressure equation while the smoothSolver with GaussSeidel controls the velocity equations. The PIMPLE algorithm is used for the pressure–velocity coupling while the implicit Euler scheme is chosen for the discretization of transient terms. Maximum Courant number is fixed at 0.5 to facilitate convergence of the solution with an adjustable time-step (default value 1 μ s ). The results obtained are validated.

Theoretical formulations developed and thoroughly researched by Doinikov and his group, have been applied for initial calculations. We have employed the code available in [6] for predicting the flow-field characteristics around a single air-bubble in water. The applied frequency and modal amplitude have been varied. This method has been applied for the initial testing and analysis at different operating conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of parameters and other flow measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
Several parameters are calculated from the CFD results. For every bubble shape, the centroid and bubble contour data (projection on a plane) are extracted and converted to polar coordinate. This is used to represent the contour on the basis of Legendre polynomial (LP),
P
n
[9,18]. Here,
r
s
( θ , t ) =
∑
n = 0

The velocity calculation is conducted by taking a temporal average of the velocity magnitude in the region of interest around the bubble. This averaging is conducted due to microstreaming being recognized as a mean flow, allowing suitable representation through average values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical effects of ultrasound on oscillating bubble
ce:section.ce:para:
As mentioned earlier, a bubble oscillating in presence of ultrasound is subjected to soundwaves that generally get reflected from all directions. This distorts the overall shape of the bubble [1]. These shape distortions can appear periodically and spread uniformly over the entire surface area of the bubble. Some of the reported shape distortions or modes are schematically shown in Fig. 4 (a). These are indicated by mode number,
n = 2 , 3 , 4 .
.. The dominant shape is accompanied by a dominant flow-pattern as well.

Such flow-patterns are characterised by the formation of primary and secondary flow vortices. These appear as circulations of the liquid immediately around a bubble. The number and nature of these flow vortices can be visualized by looking at the streamlines developing around a bubble subjected to ultrasound. These are illustrated in Fig. 4 (b). The flow-patterns shown, are produced using available mathematical codes by Regnault et al. [6]. An operating frequency of 130 kHz was considered. The resting radius of the bubble was indicated by
R
0
below each panel image. It was assumed that 5
μ m
was the amplitude of every mode for each of the above
R
0
.

The
R
0
values were calculated using the Lamb’s expression [5]. A bubble in ultrasound can be considered as a forced harmonic oscillator, with a mass associated to the inertia of the liquid, and a restoring force associated to the compressed gas. The amplitude of radial oscillations (forced at a frequency
ω
) increases with the forcing amplitude,
p
a
, and is a maximum for
R
0
=
R
r
(resonance frequency, defined in Eq. 9). Parametric instability is a phenomenon that occurs for a driving frequency
ω
n
=
ω
2
, where
ω
n
is the resonance frequency of a spherical harmonic distortion of order n (
n > 1
). The bubble tends to exibit periodic behaviour with frequency,
ω
n
[5]. This is given by the following Lamb’s expression (Eq. 10).
(10)
ω
n
2
=
( n - 1 ) ( n + 1 ) ( n + 2 ) σ
ρ
0 , l
R
r
3
Eq. 10 can further help to theoretically predict the dominant mode number for a certain
R
0
at different applied frequencies (governed by
ω
). The results from this are illustrated in Fig. 5 for two cases of low frequency ultrasound (LFUS)- 45 kHz and 130 kHz. It can be observed that with decrease in frequency,
R
0
increases and that higher mode numbers tend to appear with higher
R
0
. The same trend has been experimentally noted by Poulichet et al. [5], Cleve et al. [9], and Versluis et al. [35]. However, it is not clear what happens to the nature of the physical effects, as ultrasonic parameters are changed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ultrasonic parameters on the flow circulations
ce:section.ce:para: The same theoretical models were applied to investigate the effect of ultrasonic frequency and power amplitude on the vorticity generated in the liquid. The results are presented in Fig. 6 for the same dominant mode.

Two LFUS cases, 45 kHz and 130 kHz, are shown. The applied power is represented by the modal amplitude that has been varied as 5, 15, 25 and 35
μ m
on the bubble. Here, dominant mode number 4 is only shown. The
R
0
suitable for mode 4 has been selected from the previous Fig. 5. It can be clearly observed from here that with the increase in amplitude (from 5 to 35
μ m
), the strength of the vortices increase, irrespective of the LFUS. Also, the strength of vortices tend to increase with the increase in frequency at fixed amplitude (see the case for 15 and 25
μ m
amplitude). The blue and yellow colours help indicate the vortices and counter-vortices appearing next to each other. The bubble has been schematically excluded from the picture to focus on the flow-pattern.

It is known that higher frequency would lead to lesser bubble oscillation time [2]. Hence, it can be proposed that the flow circulations at higher values of LFUS would develop and disappear quickly as well. Not only that, the size of the bubble is also reduced with higher frequency, likely to have a similar length-scale effect on the size of the vortices. Both of these have prompted us to recreate and investigate 45 kHz bubble insonication conditions using bubble-levitation and PIV experiments. This LFUS condition can facilitate larger time and space-scale of the microstreaming effect and further help us to understand the evolution mechanism of the vortices.
As mentioned earlier, experiments were performed to capture the liquid velocity field immediately around the bubble (discussed in Sections 2.2 and 2.3).

The average of the velocity distribution in the liquid around a levitated bubble oscillating at 45 kHz is noted with time and has been represented as streaming velocity (
v
stream
). The time-history of
v
stream
is captured for two cases of acoustic power amplitude, 5.53 Vpp (
∼
0.098 MPa) and 7.02 Vpp (
∼
0.11 MPa). The results obtained are indicated in Fig. 7 (a) for a total time duration, slightly more than 2 s (indicated by the X-axis). These are plotted with markers (blue color) in Fig. 7 (a) and red colour for Fig. 7 (b). The solid lines indicate the fitted trend of the corresponding data for the two cases shown respectively. The blue colour represents the values for 7.02 Vpp (high acoustic power) and the red is for 5.53 Vpp (low acoustic power). The dotted lines in each indicate the 95
%
prediction levels, it can be observed that most of the data lie within the dotted prediction levels. The method of image collection and processing using MATLAB software has been detailed in the Supplementary Text 1 section.

Fig. 7 (a) and (b) indicate two different trends for
v
stream
with time. The mean streaming velocities show a downward trend with time for low power, while an upward trend is noticed for the high-power case. This may be because at low power, the bubbles have a tendency to dissolve. This trait was also observed by Lee et al. [26], who found that certain bubble sizes remained fixed for some time, and then slowly dissolve. We measured at much smaller time-resolutions, and observed the bubble-wall velocities attenuating till the bubble disappears in the liquid. This is also because the applied pressure here is below the threshold for bubble growth,
p
c
, commonly known as the critical pressure [1]. Conversely, for higher power (
>
p
c
), the initial bubble radius under oscillating conditions grows larger with every subsequent cycle. This is due to ”rectified diffusion” (diffusion of gases from solvent to bubble) which is known to enhance bubble growth (slow growth rate of 0.02
μ m / s
) and further intensify bubble collapse [36]. For the same applied frequency and gas content inside a bubble, larger bubbles are likely to collapse faster (indicated by higher bubble wall velocity) compared to smaller bubbles. This would hence lead to relatively higher liquid velocities. Since the bubble radius increases gradually with time, intense liquid motion is likely to occur. This might help explain the increase in velocity to 1.2 mm/s in Fig. 7(a). More details on
p
c
is provided in the Supplementary Information: experimental section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of applied pressure on streaming velocities
ce:section.ce:para:
The above observation prompts us to compare
v
stream
for three instances of acoustic power, 5.53, 6.8 and 7.02 Vpp. In each case, the
v
stream
is measured with time (
∼
few seconds), and the mean and maximum values are calculated. The spatial and temporal mean and maximum of the liquid distribution are obtained for each case of acoustic amplitude. These results are displayed in Fig. 7 (c) where mean velocities (indicated with blue coloured bars) and maximum velocities (orange markers) over all time steps are shown.

Authors have reported a threshold for shape-mode oscillation (
p
th , n
) which is the applied pressure limit above which shape-mode and microstreaming tend to occur [9].
p
th , n
measures 0.04 to 0.05 MPa for our experimental conditions. This is indicated on the horizontal X-axis in Fig. 7 (c) alongside modal amplitudes (represented by
a
n
), where n indicates the mode number.
a
n
has been schematically shown atop the vertical bar in Fig. 7 (c). Likewise, the schematic for dissolving and growing bubbles have been indicated in the same figure at 5.53 Vpp and 6.83 Vpp. This is because
p
c
has been theoretically estimated to lie between these power conditions and hence indicated on the X-axis. We further observe that
v
stream
around levitated bubble can go as high as 1 mm/s (mean), and 2.75 mm/s (max). This data is taken over an entire duration of 1 s.

In this regard, Leong et al. [15] reported streaming velocities of 0.091
±
0.005 mm/s for a substrate-attached air bubble (radius 125 micron) at 28 kHz, 3.0 kPa peak-to-peak pressure. Cleve et al. [18] observed a maximum velocity of 0.7 mm/s for acoustically trapped bubbles post-coalescence (radius 46.9 micron, dominant surface mode 2), oscillating at 180 kHz, 20.6 kPa. For our case of 7.02 Vpp, shape-mode oscillations appeared from time to time (
p
th , n
was close to 0.12 MPa [37]) while the bubble location was maintained fixed for a certain duration. Hence, flow patterns could not be captured clearly using PIV (even after overlaying several images and calculating the maximum value for each pixel as suggested by Cleve et al. [18]) but were spotted using our strobe technique. This may be one of the reasons for the existence of higher liquid velocities around the bubble. It is generally noted that bubbles attached to a surface would register high streaming velocities compared to a freely levitated bubble, which is not visible in our case. The reason for this is that velocity for our exact case was not available in the literature. Also, there is a chance of convective parasitic flows [38,18] originating from overheating of the liquid, though this has been checked by reducing the duration of measurement to eliminate any over-heating (convective flow) due to laser or ultrasonic irradiation.

Another observation from Fig. 7 (c) is that at 6.8 Vpp, a dip in
v
stream
is obtained. What we speculate here, is that the switching in the nature of bubble growth around
p c
might have led to the occurrence of some disturbances causing a reduction in
v
stream
values. Fig. 7 (d) is additionally plotted to obtain the 2.5 and 97.5 percentile information of the velocity distribution. A similar trend is also noted for the intermediate applied pressure. A possible reason may be obtained from the observations by Barber et al. [39]. They observed that with gradual change in drive pressure, there occurs a certain instance when the bubble-size (including maximum radius) shrinks and the expansion ratio increases. Hence, determining the region of lowest streaming velocity may be decisive for optimizing ultrasound applications.

For our case, difficulties are encountered during stabilizing the single bubble. Often, the centre of the bubble keeps moving erratically, till it finally becomes static (visualized with our CCD camera). Also, it is sometimes difficult to observe the bubble-shape and acquire the liquid velocity simultaneously. To overcome this, computational fluid dynamics (CFD) studies were performed to simulate the same experimental setup at 45 kHz LFUS.
Direct numerical simulation was carried out to recreate bubble oscillations at LFUS and closely understand the inter-relations between shape-mode oscillation and microstreaming.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the model: bubble shape and streaming velocity
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 8 illustrates the results from our CFD model vis-a-vis experimental snapshots of bubbles. Fig. 8 (a) shows the case of volumetric spherical oscillation of bubble below
p
cav
(Blake threshold), whereas Fig. 8(b) shows the case where bubble is above
p
th , n
, discussed from our experiments. It can be observed that there is suitable agreement between the experimental and computationally obtained images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time and space evolution of microstreaming
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 9 (a) illustrates the results from our simulation showing the shape evolutions of the bubble when subjected to LFUS (45 kHz) at around 7.02 Vpp (experiments shown earlier). The bubble measures
R
0
= 105
μ m ,
p
a
≈ 0.095
MPa.

The bubble is indicated in 3D and its three planar projections are indicated by solid lines on the three sides of the bubble (refer to the axis orientation at the left corner). The direction of the ultrasonic waves is along the positive Y axis. The time-stamps are put up at the top of each image. It can be observed that with time, the spherical shape of the bubble changes after 65
μ s
and thereafter, its surface gets dilated (see subfigure at 90
μ s
). However, this dilation appears to be uniform from all sides, based on the planar projection images. With time, the bubble keeps growing during which the surface deformations disappear (110
μ s
). But in the following collapse stage, surface dilations reappear. However, this time there appears to be an angular shift in the orientation of the deformed 4-lobed bubble (compare the projections between 95 and 145
μ s
). By 175
μ s
, the current shape-mode disappears. The surface instability is assessed by the LP decomposition (subSection 2.7). For this, the spatial coordinates from each projection are extracted at 90
μ s
, and decomposed into modal coefficients,
a
n
, against each mode number, n. The results are shown in Fig. 9 (b). It can be clearly observed that mode number,
n = 4
registers the highest contribution, typically measuring around 2.6 microns (modal amplitude). The same information can be qualitatively verified via the 4-lobed bubble shape (shown by the planar projections). Authors have reported that during microstreaming, there can be an interaction of modes,
n , m
[22]. However, exhaustive modal decomposition may help clarify this aspect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Information from the liquid pressure profile
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 10 (a) shows the pressure colourmap around the bubble with time, this is expressed in relative terms to the atmospheric pressure as
p
∗
=
p
10
5
. The sinusoidal pressure at the bottom wall rises to a maximum value of 0.19 MPa. The same pressure pulse propagates through the liquid domain in y-direction, gets reflected from the top surface and interacts with another incoming pressure pulse. It can be observed that as the bubble shrinks in shape because of the high liquid pressure outside of the bubble (90
μ s
). The bubble reaches maximum size at about 110
μ s
, when the pressure inside and outside the bubble appears to be quite uniform. Incidentally, this is when the expansion wave impinges on the bubble causing it to grow to equilibrium. In the following collapse stage, as high compression phase of the sound wave starts to build up, the bubble reduces in size and surface dilations develop. It can be observed that the bubble regains its sphericity at the end of growth stage and loses its sphericity to develop surface dilations at the end of every subsequent collapse stage. This cycle of losing and regaining sphericity continues.

Fig. 10 (b) shows the pressure values at the bubble centre and immediately above it, at different vertical locations (indicated by
y
∗
). While the bubble contents possess very high pressure, around 0.5 MPa (for
y
∗
= 0
cm), the liquid above it goes up till 0.136 MPa. The instances of pressure peaks coincide with the occurrence of minimum size of the bubble. In addition, the pressure gradients,
∂ P
∂ x
and
∂ P
∂ y
around the bubble are shown for a random time-instant in Fig. 10(c) and (d) respectively. It can be clearly observed that
∂ P
∂ x
on either side of the bubble, are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction and seem to cancel each other. However, the distribution of
∂ P
∂ y
on the same plane is clearly non-uniform on either side of the bubble in the y-direction. This non-uniformity may be because of the propagation of waves in the y-direction and partly due to scattering of the sound waves with the bubble. Further details of the pressure profile in the liquid with vertical height at different time-instants are provided in Supplementary Fig. S7. This supports the pressure fluctuations with time that accompany microstreaming.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Information from the liquid velocity profile
ce:section.ce:para:
For the corresponding applied frequency of 45 kHz, the wavelength measures close to 3 cm. When compared to the maximum bubble size (in few hundreds of microns), that exists for a few microseconds, the typical scattering would be quite negligible. Though scattering would exist, it is the pressure amplitude at the bubble location that would significantly influence the evolving bubble shapes. This can be better understood from the velocity colourmap around the bubble. This has been presented chronologically in Fig. 11 . Here, circular planes, each measuring 200
μ m
in radius from the bubble-centre are used in order to understand the region of influence around the bubble due to streaming motion. Streaming being largely a mean-flow, the field average of velocities over time till that instant is captured and presented as
|
U
mean
|
(see colourbar). While the bubble starts with weak non-zero velocity (due to bulk acoustic streaming) initially, the effect of sound waves with the oscillating bubble intensifies
|
U
mean
|
over time. The region of streaming velocity spreads well beyond the selected plane. The red coloured zones at the centre of each plane measure 0.3 m/s, which is however caused by the motion of gases inside the bubble. The velocity distributions in 110
μ s
for X and Z normal planes appear to differ largely with Y-normal plane (highest velocity at the centre). Further details of the fluid velocity with vertical height at different time-instants are provided in Supplementary Fig. S7 – this supports the velocity fluctuations with time that accompany microstreaming.

A better view of the flow or flow-direction can be obtained from the streamlines developed in the flow-field around the bubble. This is presented in Fig. 12 . Like last time, planes around the bubble are taken in the X,Y and Z-normal directions. These are projected around the bubble. A larger dimension of the plane (
300
μ m
radii) is selected this time in order to examine the flow-field farther away from the bubble. The flow-direction is indicated by the solid lines, while the colormap helps identify the vortices developing in the flow-field owing to microstreaming. Blue and red zones indicate the opposite rolls occurring near the gas–liquid interface. These flow vortices have a magnitude close to
10
5
s
- 1
. This is similar to that predicted from theoretical models as well. The vortices number 8 in total (see Fig. 12) This is characteristic of the mode
n = 4
of the bubble.

Leong et al. [41] additionally mentions the causes behind the formation of velocity gradient facilitating acoustic streaming. These include spatial attenuation of sound waves in the medium caused by absorption of energy, scattering of sound waves, or due to friction between a vibrating element and surrounding medium. Simultaneously, the liquid has a tendency to “spin” or “curl” near the oscillating bubble, leading to vorticity.

The shear stresses generated by an oscillating bubble are important for many biomedical applications e.g. drug-delivery [42], activation of bioeffects [13] or as elastomeric ultrasound contrast agent [43]. But microstreaming can play a special role in this as discussed in the following section.

The usage of low-frequency ultrasound (LFUS) in drug delivery offers a promising approach to treat various medical conditions with minimal tissue damage. Its ability to penetrate deep into tissues, coupled with its non-invasive nature and precise control over drug release, makes it a valuable tool in modern therapeutic applications [42]. However, it is important to carefully optimize ultrasound parameters for each specific application to ensure safety and efficacy. Brendan et al. [42] demonstrated the feasibility and mechanism of low-frequency ultrasound (20–60 kHz, 10–300
W /
cm
2
) on tissue removal and damage. Firstly, tissue degradation is an ongoing process, primarily initiated through contact with a probing instrument. Then the temporal dynamics of tissue degradation are characterized by notably short time constants. Additionally, it is essential to recognize that biological tissues exhibit structural heterogeneity, meaning they lack uniformity in their composition and organization.

In the study of Wischhusen [44], low-frequency ultrasound with 0.3–3
W /
cm
2
intensity can be used to improve the in vivo drug delivery. Its mechanism is related to the generated microbubbles which are introduced intravenously and circulate within the bloodstream. When subjected to ultrasound irradiation through a focused transducer, microbubbles within the tumor’s vascular network result in the disruption of intercellular junctions, perforation of cell membranes, and enhanced tissue permeability. Consequently, concurrently administered drugs, such as nanoparticles carrying therapeutic payloads, exhibit heightened ease of penetration into the tumor tissue, thereby elevating the local drug concentration. The targeted drug-delivery capabilities of microstreaming is yet to be tested and may be a suitable future work.

Nevertheless, LFUS has been proven to foster other synergistic bioeffects. Huang et al. [45] illustrated the role of cavitation in low-frequency ultrasound-mediated drug delivery. Asymmetrical transient cavitation emerges as the principal contributor to the observed skin permeabilization during low-frequency ultrasound therapy, with limited discernible involvement of symmetrical transient cavitation (as evidenced by the disparity in the relationship between ultrasound intensity and skin permeabilization as compared to symmetrical transient cavitation activity). LFUS has demonstrated the capacity to elevate the expression of endothelial nitric oxide synthase as a consequence of shear stress generated by cavitation and microstreaming phenomena. Additionally, another potential advantageous outcome associated with LFUS pertains to the induced upregulation of vascular endothelial growth factor, facilitated through the stimulation of monocytes [46].

The magnitude of shear stress due to bubble collapse may be significantly high. This is largely due to two powerful liquid vortex formations [47] adjacent to the jetting that sometimes measure as high as
6 ×
10
6
s
- 1
. Furthermore, collision of flows [48] can take place during the interaction. These are mostly one-time effect existing for some limited
μ s
. In contrast, for microstreaming the flow vortices that develop are much weaker. They number many as they arrange themselves around the bubble. Further they appear periodically with time as we see in the current study. Quite obviously, these can help generate mild values of shear stresses and also over a sustained time-period (several seconds). These characteristics of microstreaming at LFUS can help prevent permanent damage to biological tissues. LFUS has a longer wavelength, which allows them to penetrate deeper into tissues compared to higher-frequency ultrasound [42]. This deep penetration is beneficial for targeting drugs to specific internal organs or tissues. Moreover, lower frequencies result in less absorption by the tissue, reducing the risk of tissue heating and potential damage[49]. In addition, the stable and inertial cavitation generated by LFUS can be used to temporarily disrupt cell membranes or the blood–brain barrier, enhancing the permeability of tissues to drugs without causing significant damage[44]. Generally, LFUS microstreaming can be suitably controlled using
R
0
and proper insonication (frequency and power of ultrasound). This is instrumental for tissue permeabilization or lipid extraction, which is otherwise inconvenient under intense shear conditions [10,50–53]. The setup and method demonstrated along with the results of bubble-induced shear support this fact. The resulting knowledge can be used to advance ultrasonic-based technologies for more efficient and precise biomedical applications.

This study aimed to understand the nature of microstreaming caused by an unattached bubble oscillating under low frequencies based on combined results from experimental data, theoretical prediction and numerical simulations. Increase in ultrasonic frequency and power appeared to intensify the streaming effect. This was further elucidated by closely monitoring the development of streaming on a single bubble levitated at (45 ± 1) kHz. Experiments at different acoustic forcing (5.53, 6.80 and 7.02 Vpp) on a bubble helped classify a dissolving, growing and a bubble exhibiting shape-mode oscillations. Their corresponding liquid streaming velocities (mean, maximum, 2.5 percentile, 97.5 percentile) were captured with lowest (0.5 mm/s) and highest (1.1 mm/s) values of time-averaged mean streaming velocity at 6.80 and 7.02 Vpp acoustic amplitude respectively. Computational fluid dynamics model captured the bubble shape evolutions (from largest size at spherical shape during expansion to a prominently deformed shape during compression phase) revealing a dominant shape mode number for the given operating conditions. Simultaneous velocity and pressure profiles confirmed their fluctuating nature around the bubble during this phenomenon. Simulations indicated that the development of periodic surface undulations on the bubble were driven by the interaction of the bubble with the irradiated and reflected soundwaves. This work provides guidance for industry to suitably implement certain modes of microstreaming for harnessing bioeffects in LFUS.
